title: Fatigue and microcytic anemia in an older woman on an anticoagulant and chronic NSAIDs

chart.oneLiner: 67F with CKD stage 3, hypothyroidism, atrial fibrillation on apixaban, and knee osteoarthritis on chronic NSAIDs presents with months of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness; she is found to have a microcytic anemia with several concurrent metabolic abnormalities.

opening: I'm worn out all the time, doctor — even walking to the mailbox leaves me short of breath, and that's just not me.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 67-year-old woman, pale and a little tired-appearing but in no acute distress. Cooperative, reasonable historian, but does not volunteer much unless asked specifically.

HPI: Several months of gradually worsening fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, exertional shortness of breath, and intermittent lightheadedness when she stands quickly. No chest pain, no palpitations beyond her known irregular heartbeat, no syncope. On direct questioning: her stools have looked darker than usual on and off over the last couple of months, though she has not seen frank blood; no obvious black tarry stools that she has noticed. NO weight loss, NO night sweats, NO fevers. NO hematemesis. She is post-menopausal (last period ~15 years ago) with NO vaginal bleeding. She takes ibuprofen most days for her knee arthritis and has done so for years. She has noticed she sometimes craves and chews ice — only if asked about cravings.
PMH (reveal when asked about medical history): chronic kidney disease stage 3 (told her kidneys are 'about half'), hypothyroidism, atrial fibrillation, knee osteoarthritis, GERD, and a stomach ulcer related to a germ ('H. pylori') treated a few years ago. No known prior anemia, no prior transfusions, no cancer.
MEDS (reveal when asked about medications): apixaban (a blood thinner) for atrial fibrillation; ibuprofen most days for knee pain (over-the-counter, takes it on her own); omeprazole for reflux; levothyroxine for thyroid; metoprolol for heart rate; acetaminophen as needed. NO recent antibiotics. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: retired teacher, lives with her husband. Diet light on red meat (does not enjoy it), mostly vegetables and dairy. Rare alcohol (a glass of wine on holidays). Never smoked. FAMILY: a sister with thyroid disease; father had colon cancer in his 70s. ROS: as above — fatigue, exertional dyspnea, lightheadedness, darker stools intermittently; no urinary symptoms, no joint swelling, no easy bruising beyond what she expects on the blood thinner.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, comfortable, well-nourished.
- Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 92 and irregularly irregular, RR 18, 36.7°C, SpO2 97% RA. Orthostatics if obtained: drops to 112/72 with HR rise to 104 on standing, mildly symptomatic.
- HEENT: conjunctival and mucous-membrane pallor; smooth/atrophic tongue if examined; angular cheilosis if inspected.
- Nails: koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) if examined.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, soft early systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border, no rub, no gallop.
- Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally.
- Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no rebound or guarding, no hepatosplenomegaly, no mass.
- Rectal (if examined): dark/brown stool that is guaiac-positive; no gross melena, no mass palpable.
- Extremities: trace ankle edema, no calf tenderness.
- Thyroid: non-tender, no goiter if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the darker stools, the daily ibuprofen, the apixaban, the prior H. pylori ulcer, the ice craving, or the family history of colon cancer unless asked about the relevant topic (stools/bleeding, medications, past ulcers, cravings, or family history).
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant maneuver is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 8.4, MCV 71 (low — microcytic), MCH 23 (low — hypochromic), RDW 18.5 (high), WBC 6.8 (normal), Plt 472 (mild reactive thrombocytosis). Anemia is microcytic and hypochromic with marked anisocytosis.
labs.Smear: Microcytic, hypochromic red cells with anisopoikilocytosis and classic pencil (cigar) cells; occasional target cells; no basophilic stippling, no schistocytes, no blasts. Picture favors iron deficiency over a thalassemic process.
labs.IronStudies: Ferritin 7 ng/mL (LOW), serum iron low, TIBC HIGH (elevated), transferrin saturation 6% (LOW) — a classic absolute iron-deficiency pattern (low ferritin with a high TIBC argues against anemia of chronic disease, in which ferritin is normal/high and TIBC is low).
labs.Reticulocyte: Reticulocyte count low / inappropriately normal for the degree of anemia (reticulocyte production index < 2) — hypoproliferative marrow consistent with iron-deficient erythropoiesis.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 4.6, Cl 104, HCO3 24 — anion gap = 139 − (104 + 24) = 11 (NORMAL, no acidosis). BUN 32, creatinine 1.6 (baseline ~1.5; eGFR ~36 — stable CKD stage 3, not acutely worse). Glucose 98. Calcium 9.3. Electrolytes internally consistent; the anemia is NOT explained by uremia alone.
labs.TFTs: TSH 8.9 (HIGH), free T4 low-normal — mild undertreated/subclinical-to-overt hypothyroidism, a SEPARATE concurrent problem that can itself cause fatigue and a mild normo-/macrocytic anemia, but does NOT explain a microcytic iron-deficiency picture.
labs.FOBT_Hpylori: Fecal occult blood test POSITIVE (guaiac-positive stool on rectal exam, confirmed on lab FOBT). Stool H. pylori antigen POSITIVE — active H. pylori, a treatable source of upper-GI mucosal blood loss, layered on top of chronic NSAID use and apixaban anticoagulation.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm at ~90–100 bpm with absent P waves and a fibrillating baseline; controlled ventricular response. No acute ischemic ST/T changes. This is the patient's OWN known atrial fibrillation — relevant because she is anticoagulated on apixaban, which bears on her GI blood loss.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation, irregularly irregular, absent P waves, controlled rate, no acute ischemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemia
searchTerms: atrial fibrillation 12 lead ECG controlled rate
irregularly irregular absent P waves ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: An older woman with palpitations and an irregular pulse on examination; she is on an oral anticoagulant.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette; no effusion, no consolidation, no overt cardiomegaly. A reassuring film that does not explain the anemia or the dyspnea (which is the anemia itself).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no effusion. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: PA chest radiograph with clear lungs and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette
searchTerms: clear lung fields normal cardiomediastinal silhouette radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: An older woman with exertional dyspnea has a screening chest film that is unremarkable.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this older woman on an anticoagulant and chronic NSAIDs, give your differential for this microcytic anemia (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly name at least three mechanisms/etiologies by which she could be iron-deficient, and state which competing cause of microcytic anemia you must actively exclude.
steps.rubric: Iron deficiency anemia (leading,5). Anemia of chronic disease / CKD-related anemia — the genuine competitor in an older CKD patient (CANT-MISS to distinguish,4). Occult GI blood loss with underlying malignancy (colon/gastric cancer) given age + family history — must-not-miss source (CANT-MISS,4). Thalassemia trait (core,2). Sideroblastic anemia (bonus,2). Names ≥3 mechanisms/etiologies of iron deficiency here — NSAID/anticoagulant-related GI mucosal blood loss, H. pylori-related ulcer loss, poor dietary intake (low red meat), and impaired absorption from chronic PPI use (core,5). States that ferritin + TIBC are the discriminator that separates iron deficiency from anemia of chronic disease (core,3). PENALTY: attributing the anemia solely to CKD/'anemia of chronic disease' and not pursuing a bleeding source (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Iron deficiency anemia (microcytic, hypochromic, pencil cells, ice craving). The genuine competing diagnosis to exclude is anemia of chronic disease / CKD-related anemia — she has stage 3 CKD, so both can give a low-normal-MCV anemia; the iron studies (low ferritin, HIGH TIBC, low transferrin saturation) distinguish absolute iron deficiency (here) from ACD (normal/high ferritin, low TIBC). The must-not-miss is occult GI blood loss from a malignancy (colon or gastric cancer) given her age and a father with colon cancer. At least three mechanisms/etiologies of her iron deficiency: (1) chronic NSAID-induced gastric/duodenal mucosal blood loss, amplified by (2) apixaban anticoagulation, (3) active H. pylori ulcer disease, plus (4) reduced dietary iron intake (little red meat) and (5) impaired iron absorption from chronic PPI/achlorhydria. Also reasonable: thalassemia trait and sideroblastic anemia.

criticalActions.item: Anemia of chronic disease / CKD-related anemia (the competing cause to exclude)
Occult GI blood loss with underlying malignancy (colon/gastric cancer)

coreActions.item: Names ≥3 mechanisms/etiologies of iron deficiency (NSAID/anticoagulant loss, H. pylori ulcer, low intake, impaired PPI absorption)
States ferritin + TIBC distinguish iron deficiency from anemia of chronic disease

penalties.item: Attributing the anemia solely to CKD/ACD without pursuing a bleeding source

mustNotMiss: Occult GI blood loss with underlying malignancy (colon/gastric cancer)
Anemia of chronic disease / CKD-related anemia (the competing cause to exclude)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order to characterize the anemia, identify the source, and account for her comorbidities?
steps.rubric: CBC with indices and RDW (CANT-MISS,3). Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation (CANT-MISS,4). Fecal occult blood test + H. pylori testing (CANT-MISS,3). Peripheral smear (1). Reticulocyte count (1). BMP to assess CKD and rule uremia in/out (1). TSH given fatigue and known hypothyroidism (1). Recognizes GI endoscopy (EGD ± colonoscopy) is the definitive source work-up in this older anticoagulated patient (1). PENALTY: transfusing a hemodynamically stable, chronically anemic patient with no active bleeding (-2); ordering iron studies AFTER giving iron (which falsely normalizes ferritin) (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC with indices and RDW; peripheral smear; iron studies (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation) BEFORE any iron is given; reticulocyte count; BMP (assess her CKD/uremia); TSH (fatigue + hypothyroidism); fecal occult blood test and H. pylori testing; and plan GI endoscopy (EGD and colonoscopy) as the definitive search for the bleeding source in an older anticoagulated woman. Also EKG and CXR.

criticalActions.item: Iron studies (ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation) before giving iron
Fecal occult blood test + H. pylori testing

coreActions.item: BMP to assess CKD / exclude uremia
Plan GI endoscopy (EGD ± colonoscopy) for the source

penalties.item: Transfusing a stable, chronically anemic patient with no active bleeding
Ordering iron studies AFTER giving iron

requiredTests: Iron studies (ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation) before giving iron
Fecal occult blood test + H. pylori testing
optionalTests: BMP to assess CKD / exclude uremia
Plan GI endoscopy (EGD ± colonoscopy) for the source
contraindicatedTests: Transfusing a stable, chronically anemic patient with no active bleeding
Ordering iron studies AFTER giving iron
orderSensitiveActions: Iron studies (ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation) before giving iron

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they bear on her anemia work-up.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response (~90–100), absent P waves, irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm, no acute ischemic ST/T changes. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation with controlled rate (7); notes absent P waves / irregularly irregular rhythm and no acute ischemia (5); connects the AF to her apixaban anticoagulation as a contributor to GI blood loss (3). PENALTY: mislabeling as multifocal atrial tachycardia or sinus rhythm, or over-reading ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: absent P waves with an irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm
controlled ventricular response, no acute ischemic changes
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes — her known AF, the reason she is anticoagulated on apixaban.
commonMistakes: calling an irregularly irregular rhythm multifocal atrial tachycardia or sinus arrhythmia
over-reading benign T-wave changes as ischemia
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm at ~90–100 bpm with absent P waves and a fibrillating baseline; controlled ventricular response; no acute ST/T ischemic changes. This is her chronic AF, and it matters because it is why she takes apixaban — anticoagulation that is contributing to her occult GI blood loss and that must be weighed against her bleeding when planning management.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes — her known AF, the reason she is anticoagulated on apixaban.
coreActions: absent P waves with an irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm
controlled ventricular response, no acute ischemic changes

penalties.label: mislabels the rhythm or over-reads ischemia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no effusion or consolidation, no overt cardiomegaly. CREDIT: recognizes a normal/reassuring film (5); states it does NOT explain her dyspnea, which is from the anemia itself (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology on a clear film, or attributing the dyspnea to an imagined cardiopulmonary process (-2).
keyFindings: clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette
no effusion, consolidation, or overt cardiomegaly
criticalFindings: Clear, reassuring chest film — no cardiopulmonary cause for the dyspnea, which is explained by the anemia.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion on a clear film
attributing the exertional dyspnea to a primary cardiopulmonary process
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette and no effusion, consolidation, or overt cardiomegaly. The film is reassuring and does NOT explain her exertional dyspnea — that symptom is the anemia itself.

criticalActions: Clear, reassuring chest film — no cardiopulmonary cause for the dyspnea, which is explained by the anemia.
coreActions: clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette
no effusion, consolidation, or overt cardiomegaly

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the iron studies, smear, FOBT/H. pylori results, the BMP and TSH, and her atrial fibrillation on the EKG, commit to your diagnosis and the likely source. Name the discriminating test that settled iron deficiency versus anemia of chronic disease, and address how her concurrent abnormalities (CKD, hypothyroidism, AF/anticoagulation) fit in.
steps.rubric: Absolute iron deficiency anemia confirmed by a LOW ferritin (7) with a HIGH TIBC and low transferrin saturation — the discriminator that excludes anemia of chronic disease/CKD as the primary cause (6). Identifies the source as occult upper-GI blood loss from chronic NSAID use + active H. pylori, amplified by apixaban anticoagulation (seen as her AF on the EKG); recognizes the high TSH (hypothyroidism) and stable CKD as SEPARATE concurrent problems that contribute to fatigue but do not explain a microcytic, iron-deficient picture; commits to iron deficiency while still requiring endoscopy to exclude malignancy (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Absolute iron deficiency anemia. The discriminating test is the iron panel: a LOW ferritin (7) with a HIGH TIBC and a 6% transferrin saturation proves true iron deficiency and excludes anemia of chronic disease/CKD as the primary cause (in ACD ferritin would be normal/high and TIBC low). The pencil cells on smear support iron deficiency over thalassemia. The likely source is occult upper-GI blood loss from chronic NSAID use plus active H. pylori (positive stool antigen and FOBT), amplified by her apixaban anticoagulation — the apixaban is for the atrial fibrillation visible on her EKG. Her other abnormalities are concurrent but separate: the HIGH TSH (hypothyroidism) and her stable stage 3 CKD both contribute to fatigue but cannot explain a microcytic, iron-deficient anemia. I commit to iron deficiency anemia but still require GI endoscopy (EGD and colonoscopy) to exclude an occult malignancy given her age and family history.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including how you handle her anticoagulation and NSAIDs, and your disposition/safe-discharge decision for this older patient?
steps.rubric: Per MGH anemia work-up: iron repletion — oral ferrous sulfate (every-other-day dosing improves absorption) or IV iron given her CKD/PPI-impaired absorption and ongoing loss (CANT-MISS,5); identify and treat the source — GI endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) to find the bleeding lesion and exclude malignancy (CANT-MISS,5); STOP the NSAID and treat H. pylori with eradication therapy + continue PPI (CANT-MISS,4); review/manage apixaban with cardiology — do NOT blindly continue full-dose anticoagulation through active GI loss; weigh stroke risk vs bleeding (3); transfuse only if symptomatic/unstable — she is stable, so no transfusion now (2); treat the concurrent hypothyroidism (adjust levothyroxine) and recheck CBC/reticulocyte response (2). DISPOSITION/SAFE DISCHARGE: safe for outpatient management with arranged outpatient endoscopy, medication reconciliation (stop NSAID, hold/adjust anticoagulation), strict return precautions for melena/hematochezia/lightheadedness, and close PCP/GI follow-up (3). PENALTY: continuing the NSAID and full-dose apixaban without addressing the bleeding (-3); discharging without arranging endoscopy / source work-up (-3); transfusing a stable patient without indication (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Repenish iron — oral ferrous sulfate with every-other-day dosing to improve absorption, or IV iron given her CKD, chronic PPI-related malabsorption, and ongoing losses. Find and treat the source: arrange GI endoscopy (EGD AND colonoscopy) to localize the bleeding lesion and exclude an occult malignancy. STOP the daily ibuprofen, start H. pylori eradication therapy, and continue the PPI. Review her apixaban with cardiology — do not blindly continue full-dose anticoagulation through active GI blood loss; balance her stroke risk against bleeding (consider holding/adjusting until the source is controlled). She is hemodynamically stable, so transfuse only if she becomes symptomatic or unstable — no transfusion now. Optimize her hypothyroidism (adjust levothyroxine for the high TSH) and recheck CBC and reticulocytes to confirm response. DISPOSITION: she is safe for outpatient management — arrange outpatient endoscopy, perform medication reconciliation (stop NSAID, hold/adjust anticoagulation, continue PPI), give strict return precautions for black/bloody stools, worsening lightheadedness, or chest pain, and ensure close PCP, GI, and cardiology follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Iron repletion (oral every-other-day ferrous sulfate or IV iron)
Identify and treat the source — GI endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) to exclude malignancy
Stop the NSAID and treat H. pylori with eradication therapy + continue PPI

coreActions.item: Review/manage apixaban with cardiology — weigh stroke vs bleeding, do not blindly continue full-dose anticoagulation
Transfuse only if symptomatic/unstable (she is stable — no transfusion now)
Treat concurrent hypothyroidism and recheck CBC/reticulocyte response
Safe discharge: outpatient endoscopy arranged, medication reconciliation, return precautions, close PCP/GI/cardiology follow-up

penalties.item: Continuing the NSAID and full-dose apixaban without addressing the bleeding
Discharging without arranging endoscopy / source work-up
Transfusing a stable patient without indication

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 67F with stage 3 CKD, hypothyroidism, atrial fibrillation on apixaban, and knee osteoarthritis on chronic NSAIDs presents with months of fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness and is found to have a microcytic, hypochromic anemia. The diagnosis is Iron deficiency anemia from occult GI blood loss (chronic NSAID use plus active H. pylori, amplified by anticoagulation), confirmed by a low ferritin with a high TIBC. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (darker stools, OTC NSAIDs, anticoagulant, prior ulcer, family colon cancer), performing the targeted exam, and integrating multi-system labs while distinguishing iron deficiency from the competing anemia of chronic disease/CKD and excluding occult malignancy.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: HEENT: conjunctival and mucous-membrane pallor; smooth/atrophic tongue if examined; angular cheilosis if inspected.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, soft early systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border, no rub, no gallop.
Orthostatics: BP drops to 112/72 with HR rise to 104 on standing, mildly symptomatic — consistent with the degree of anemia.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the rich comorbidity and polypharmacy.
Elicit the focused history, including darker stools, daily OTC ibuprofen, apixaban, the prior H. pylori ulcer, and the family colon cancer only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers (pallor, koilonychia, orthostatics, irregular rhythm) to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Iron deficiency anemia while actively distinguishing the competing anemia of chronic disease/CKD and naming the must-not-miss occult GI malignancy.
Integrate multi-system labs (iron studies, FOBT/H. pylori, BMP/CKD, TSH) and read the patient's own atrial fibrillation on the EKG and a reassuring CXR.
Commit to Iron deficiency anemia, identify and treat the bleeding source, manage anticoagulation/NSAIDs, and arrange safe outpatient disposition with endoscopy and follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Attributing the anemia solely to CKD/anemia of chronic disease
Failing to pursue the bleeding source / arrange endoscopy
Continuing the NSAID and full-dose anticoagulation through active GI loss
Transfusing a stable patient without indication
managementPearls: Iron repletion (oral every-other-day ferrous sulfate or IV iron)
Identify and treat the source — GI endoscopy, stop NSAID, eradicate H. pylori, review anticoagulation
criticalWorkupItems: Iron studies (ferritin/iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation) before giving iron
Fecal occult blood test + H. pylori testing
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes — her known AF, the reason she is anticoagulated.
Clear, reassuring chest film — no cardiopulmonary cause for the dyspnea, which is explained by the anemia.
criticalManagementItems: Iron repletion (oral every-other-day ferrous sulfate or IV iron)
Identify and treat the source — GI endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) to exclude malignancy
Stop the NSAID and treat H. pylori with eradication therapy + continue PPI
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes.
Clear, reassuring chest film with no cardiopulmonary cause for the dyspnea.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes.
Clear, reassuring chest film with no cardiopulmonary cause for the dyspnea.
imageInterpretationPearls: An integrated tracing is part of the puzzle — her atrial fibrillation explains the apixaban that is feeding her GI blood loss.
Read every study systematically before committing.
A normal chest film is itself informative: the exertional dyspnea is the anemia, not a cardiopulmonary process.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (stools, alcohol, family cancer)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
